key_insight: Transition-metal substitution turns a weakly sensing Ga2O2 monolayer into a tunable platform for room-temperature gas detection or permanent toxic-gas scavenging.
engineering_relevance: This offers a scalable design route for atmospheric monitoring and environmental detoxification using atomically engineered 2D materials.
method: Performed first-principles DFT calculations on Ga2O2 monolayers substituted with Pd, Zn, Zr, Mo, Ag, Ti, and Pt.
Checked structural stability using formation/binding energies, lattice distortion, and molecular dynamics.
Evaluated adsorption and electronic response against eight toxic industrial gases and three environmental gases.
evidence_signal: Across the tested gases, the substituted monolayers showed strong selectivity and performance shifts, including up to an eight-order-of-magnitude conductivity enhancement for Ag-substituted Ga2O2 with NO adsorption.
watch_item: The conclusions are computational and depend on idealized adsorption conditions, so real-device performance may change with defects, humidity, and competing ambient gases.
paper_title: Transition-Metal Tailored $Ga_{2}O_{2}$ Monolayer: From Room-Temperature Gas Sensing to Chemical Scavenging
